name: Jotunheim
description: Jotunheim, also known as Jötunheimr, is one of the Nine Worlds in Norse cosmology, and is the homeland of the giants (jötunn). It is typically depicted as a realm of wild, untamed wilderness, characterized by dense forests, jagged mountains, and icy rivers. Often located to the east of Midgard (the realm of humans), Jotunheim represents the forces of chaos and destruction in opposition to the order maintained by the gods of Asgard. The giants who dwell there are powerful and often hostile to the gods and humans, frequently engaging in conflicts and scheming against them. The realm is a source of both fear and fascination in Norse mythology, embodying the raw, untamed power of nature. Notable locations within Jotunheim include Utgard, a fortress ruled by Utgarda-Loki, and the forest of Ironwood, home to giantesses and monstrous wolves. Encounters with the giants in Jotunheim often serve as trials for gods and heroes, testing their strength, cunning, and resilience. The landscape itself is a formidable opponent, requiring exceptional endurance and skill to navigate. Jotunheim symbolizes the constant struggle between order and chaos, a central theme in Norse mythology.
subtitle: Land of the Giants

keyMyths.title: Thor's Journey to Utgard
keyMyths.description: Thor, Loki, and Thialfi journey to Utgard where they are tricked and humiliated by Utgarda-Loki, demonstrating the cunning and power of the giants.

keyMyths.title: The Theft of Idunn's Apples
keyMyths.description: The giant Thjazi kidnaps Idunn and her apples of youth, causing the gods to age until Loki retrieves her by transforming into a falcon.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Jotunheim and its inhabitants.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, including descriptions of Jotunheim and its inhabitants.
